methods.description: Statistical procedures used to analyze the collected aerobiological and meteorological data, including trend estimation using linear regression and testing statistical dependence between pollen season characteristics and meteorological variables using Spearman correlation.

steps.description: To estimate the increasing or decreasing trends of the aerobiological and meteorological parameters, we performed linear regression analysis.
steps.searchable_summary: perform linear regression to estimate trends in time series data
outputs: linear regression model results (e.g., slope, p-value, R-squared) for trends

steps.description: Spearman correlation analyses were conducted between the MPS characteristics (start, end and length dates of the MPS, pollen integral, pollen peak concentration and pollen peak day) and the meteorological variables (maximum, minimum and average temperatures, rainfall and relative humidity) to test the statistical dependence.
steps.searchable_summary: calculate Spearman correlation between two sets of variables
inputs: Main Pollen Season (MPS) characteristics (start date, end date, length, pollen integral, peak concentration, peak day)
meteorological variables (maximum temperature, minimum temperature, average temperature, rainfall, relative humidity)

steps.description: For the statistical analyses, IBM SPSS Statistics 24.0 software was applied.
steps.searchable_summary: specify software used for statistical analysis
inputs: statistical analysis tasks (linear regression, Spearman correlation)

methods.method_name: Airborne Pollen Data Collection and Processing
methods.description: Methodology for collecting airborne pollen data using volumetric traps, processing samples according to a standardized protocol, calculating daily pollen concentrations and Annual Pollen Integral (API), and determining Main Pollen Season (MPS) parameters using specialized software and a specific modeling approach.

steps.description: Airborne pollen was collected using Lanzoni VPPS-2000 volumetric traps [32], from 1995 to 2019 in Vigo and Ourense (Northwest Iberian Peninsula) and from 2003 to 2019 in Toledo (central Iberian Peninsula).
steps.searchable_summary: collect airborne pollen data using volumetric traps over specified periods and locations
outputs: raw pollen samples collected on filters or tapes

steps.description: The samples obtained were processed following the standardized methodology of the Spanish Aerobiological Network [33].
steps.searchable_summary: process raw pollen samples according to a standard methodology
outputs: processed pollen samples (e.g., prepared slides or filters ready for analysis)

steps.description: Pollen data were expressed as pollen grains per cubic meter of air for the Annual Pollen Integral (API) and the daily pollen concentrations [34].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate daily pollen concentration and annual pollen integral from processed data
inputs: processed pollen data (e.g., counts from microscopy)
air volume sampled by the trap

steps.description: To calculate the Main Pollen Season (MPS) parameters, we used the AeRobiology software package [35].
steps.searchable_summary: calculate main pollen season parameters using specified software
outputs: Main Pollen Season (MPS) parameters (start date, end date, length, pollen integral within MPS, peak concentration, peak day)

steps.description: The MPS was calculated using a pollen emission model that fits a nonlinear logistic regression model to the values of the accumulated sum of daily airborne pollen concentration sampled over a year [36,37].
steps.searchable_summary: fit a nonlinear logistic regression model to accumulated daily pollen sum to determine MPS
inputs: accumulated sum of daily airborne pollen concentration over a year
outputs: Main Pollen Season (MPS) parameters (derived from the fitted model)
